1: "You shall not see your brother's ox or his sheep going astray and ignore them (hitalamta, H5956). You shall take them back to your brother" — hitalamta (H5956, to hide oneself, to pretend not to see): the sin of studied inattention to a neighbor's need. The law prohibits not just theft but indifference. The obligation to return lost property extends to all livestock found wandering without their owner, even if the owner is an enemy (Exod 23:4-5 adds the enemy stipulation). The covenant creates an economy of mutual care: the neighbor's loss is my responsibility to remedy. The anti-indifference principle underlies the Good Samaritan parable (Luke 10:30-37: the Levite who "passed by on the other side" violates the Deuteronomic spirit).
2: If the owner is far away or unknown, you shall bring the animal home and keep it until the owner comes looking for it — the burden of temporary stewardship: Israel's law creates a system of responsible custody for lost property rather than the opportunistic appropriation of found goods. The temporary keeping (asafta el-tokh beitecha) is the social infrastructure that assumes community trust and assumed return. The law implicitly prohibits the "finders keepers" principle in favor of the "steward until the owner appears" principle.
3: The same law applies to a donkey, a garment, or anything lost by a neighbor — the enumeration broadens the principle beyond livestock to all lost property. The specificity of examples (ox, sheep, donkey, garment, anything) ensures no category of property is exempted by the narrowness of the law's letter. The garment-inclusion is significant: clothing is intimate, personal, and often representing identity — its loss and return is an act of covenant solidarity.
4: "You shall not see your brother's donkey or his ox fallen down by the way and ignore them. You shall help him to lift them up" — the active assistance command: not only return of lost property but physical help with a neighbor's struggling animal. The repetition of hitalamta (hide yourself/ignore) from v1 frames both laws: the prohibition is the same indifferent non-response. The "lift up with him" (hakem taqim imo) requires cooperation — this is not a solo task but a communal effort. The double infinitive absolute (hakem taqim) emphasizes the certainty of the obligation.
5: "A woman shall not wear a man's garment, nor shall a man put on a woman's cloak, for whoever does these things is an abomination (toevah, H8441) to the LORD your God" — the boundary legislation: keli gever (man's vessel/article) is the broad term for what belongs to the male domain. The toevah term (usually reserved for idolatry and sexual sin) marks this as a boundary violation of high seriousness. The law resists the dissolution of the male-female distinction created in Genesis 1:27 — the sexual differentiation of image-bearers is a creation order that the covenant protects. The specific social context (Canaanite cultic cross-dressing practices in fertility worship) may shape the law's intensity.
6: "If you come across a bird's nest in any tree or on the ground, with young ones or eggs and the mother sitting on the young or on the eggs, you shall not take the mother with the young" — the bird nest law: a remarkably tender environmental-ethical instruction interrupting the surrounding social legislation. The mother bird must be released before taking the young. The law's logic includes: (1) ecological sustainability (not destroying breeding pairs); (2) mercy as a character-forming practice extended to non-human life; (3) the covenant's concern for creation-care built into everyday activity.
7: "You shall let the mother go, but the young you may take for yourself, that it may go well with you, and that you may live long" — lema'an yitav lach veha'arachta yamim (that it may go well with you and you may prolong days): precisely the life-extension promise attached to honoring parents (Deut 5:16; Eph 6:2-3). The parallelism is theologically significant: mercy shown to a mother bird receives the same promised reward as mercy shown to one's mother. The covenant creates a continuity of compassion across the human-animal boundary. Matthew 23:37: "How often I would have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings" — Jesus uses the mother-bird image as the paradigm of his own pastoral desire for Jerusalem, invoking the Deuteronomic mercy-toward-mother-bird in his own mission's framework.
8: "When you build a new house, you shall make a parapet for your roof, that you may not bring the guilt of blood upon your house, if anyone should fall from it" — the building code: a parapet (H4624, maaqeh) on flat roofs prevents accidental death. The law creates personal liability for preventable death: not just murder but negligence that results in death makes one guilty (dam, blood-guilt). The rooftop was a place of daily life in ancient Israelite homes — sleeping, drying grain, social gathering. The safety requirement reflects covenant care for life extended to architecture: your home must not be a hazard to others.
9: "You shall not sow your vineyard with two kinds of seed, lest the whole yield be forfeited (tiqdash, H6942), the crop that you have sown and the yield of the vineyard" — mixed seeds (kil'ayim, H3610, two kinds): the prohibition of mixing is a category-purity principle that runs through Deuteronomy 22 (mixed seeds, mixed yoking, mixed fibers). The rationale is debated: (1) creation-order preservation — each species reproduces after its kind (Gen 1:11-12); (2) symbolic distinction between Israel (YHWH's planting) and the nations (different seeds). The forfeiture consequence (tiqdash, the whole becomes holy/set apart, unusable for common use) makes the violation self-defeating: you lose the entire harvest by mixing.
10: "You shall not plow with an ox and a donkey together" — the mixed-yoking prohibition: two different animals yoked together creates unequal strain (the ox's heavier pull vs. the donkey's lighter trot) and is cruel to the weaker animal. But the law also participates in the same categorical-distinction logic as v9. 2 Corinthians 6:14: "Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers." Paul's application of the mixed-yoking image to the covenant community's relationships — partnerships, marriages, business — follows the Deuteronomic principle: different kinds functioning under the same yoke create distortion, cruelty, and inefficiency for both.
11: "You shall not wear cloth of wool and linen mixed together" — sha'atnez (H8162, the mixed fabric): another kil'ayim prohibition. Wool (animal-derived) and linen (plant-derived) must not be woven together. The law is one of the chuqqim (statutes without explicitly stated reason) that Deuteronomy preserves from Sinai. The paradox is that the high priest's garments and the tabernacle curtains did use wool and linen together — marking those sacred items as uniquely set apart from the common domain. The prohibition for ordinary Israelites may thus distinguish common life from sacred space.
12: "You shall make yourself tassels (gedilim, H1434) on the four corners of the garment with which you cover yourself" — the tassel command: gedilim (braided/twisted tassels) are the Deuteronomic version of the Numbers 15:38-40 tzitzit with the blue cord (petil tekhelet). The tassels are embodied memory devices: "you shall see it and remember all the commandments" (Num 15:39). Matthew 9:20: "And behold, a woman who had suffered from a discharge of blood for twelve years came up behind him and touched the fringe (kraspedon, LXX translates tzitzit with this word) of his garment." The woman's touch of Jesus's tzitzit-tassel is a touch of the covenant sign: she reaches out to the one who embodies the law's fulfillment, and healing flows from that touch. Mark 6:56: crowds begged to touch "the fringe of his garment" — the tassel-touch as faith-gesture throughout Jesus's ministry.
13: "If any man takes a wife and goes in to her and then hates her and accuses her of misconduct and brings a bad name upon her" — the beginning of the marriage-dispute legislation (vv13-21): the law creates a judicial process for accusations of non-virginity at marriage. The husband's accusation must be adjudicated by the town elders with specific evidence required. The law protects women from defamation: a false accusation carries penalty (v18-19). The "bad name" (shem ra) is a legal concept — damaging someone's reputation requires judicial remedy, not merely social consequence.
14: "I did not find this woman to be a virgin" — the specific accusation. The evidence in question is the "evidence of virginity" (betulim, H1331): in the ancient context, the bloodstained cloth from the wedding night preserved as communal proof. The system is deeply embedded in ancient honor-culture practices. The law's purpose is to prevent the arbitrary dismissal or defamation of wives by taking the accusation out of private resolution into judicial process — a protection for women in a context where false accusation could be fatal.
15: The father and mother shall take the evidence of virginity and bring it to the town elders at the gate — the gate (sha'ar) as judicial space: the city gate is consistently the location of legal proceedings in Israel (Ruth 4:1-12; Amos 5:12,15; Prov 31:23). The evidence is presented to the public authority, not decided privately. The parents' role as advocates for their daughter — bringing the evidence before the elders — is a procedural protection: the woman has legal representation through her family.
16: "I gave my daughter to this man to marry, and he hates her" — the father's advocacy statement. Sane (H8130, hate) in Deuteronomy's marriage laws often functions as a quasi-legal term for the desire to dissolve a marriage (Deut 24:3: "if the latter man hates her"). The husband's emotion is a legal category that triggers specific legal consequences: dislike alone does not justify defamation.
17: "And now he has accused her of misconduct, saying, 'I did not find your daughter a virgin.' Yet here is the evidence of my daughter's virginity" — the presentation of evidence. The father's formal challenge: the accusation is legally disputed with physical evidence. The law establishes that accusation must meet counter-evidence; the elder-court adjudicates between the two. The procedural protection prevents naked accusation from carrying automatic legal weight.
18: Then the elders of that town shall take the man and whip him — the penalty for false accusation: the man who defamed his wife is punished corporally. The whipping and the financial penalty (v19: a hundred shekels of silver to the father) are substantial sanctions. The law's logic is symmetrical to the penalty it would impose if the accusation were true (v21: the woman would die): false accusation of a capital offense receives serious punishment, though not matching in kind.
19: "And they shall fine him a hundred shekels of silver and give them to the father of the young woman, because he has brought a bad name upon a virgin of Israel. And she shall be his wife. He may not divorce her all his days" — the compounded consequence: financial penalty + permanent loss of the right to divorce. The man who weaponized the divorce threat through defamation loses the divorce option entirely. The law creates a permanent bond as both protection for the woman and punishment for the man — an unusual covenant innovation that makes the consequence of the sin the structure of the remedy.
20: But if the thing is true — evidence of virginity not found — then the woman shall be brought to the door of her father's house and stoned to death — the capital consequence: the woman who entered marriage having violated her betrothal is guilty of the same offense as adultery (because betrothal was legally binding in Israel — cf. Matt 1:18-19: Joseph's discovery before their marriage required a "divorce" certificate to end the betrothal). The severity reflects the covenant's understanding of sexual faithfulness as a covenant matter, not merely a personal choice.
21: "She has done an outrageous thing (nevalah, H5039) in Israel by whoring in her father's house" — nevalah (H5039, outrage/disgrace): a term for acts that violate the social-ethical fabric of the covenant community in a deeply destructive way. The same term is used for Dinah's rape (Gen 34:7), the Gibeah gang-rape (Judg 19:23), Amnon's rape of Tamar (2 Sam 13:12). The father's house is the domain of covenantal oversight: the violation happened under the father's responsibility. "So you shall purge the evil from your midst" (uvi'arta hara miqirbecha) — the standard Deuteronomic formula for removing covenant-corrupting sin from the community.
22: "If a man is found lying with the wife of another man, both of them shall die, the man who lay with the woman, and the woman" — the adultery law: both parties receive equal penalty. The symmetrical punishment contrasts with ancient Near Eastern laws that typically punished only the woman. Deuteronomy's equal application prevents the law from being used as a tool of gender-selective punishment. Jesus in John 8:1-11 (the woman caught in adultery): the Pharisees bring only the woman, violating the Deuteronomic symmetry — the absent man is itself a problem with their application. Jesus's response ("let the one without sin cast the first stone") is consistent with the law's requirement for dual-guilt prosecution.
23: "If there is a betrothed virgin, and a man meets her in the city and lies with her" — the rape-or-consensual-sex-in-city law: the legal presumption when the encounter occurs in the city is that the woman could have called for help and did not. The presumption of urban accessibility to help creates the legal inference of consent when no cry was raised. The law is making a probabilistic legal inference, not a moral judgment about all possible scenarios — it establishes the burden of evidence for determining consent in the pre-forensic legal context.
24: Both shall be brought to the gate of the city and stoned to death — in the city scenario: both guilty, both sentenced. "The young woman because she did not cry for help though she was in the city, and the man because he violated his neighbor's wife" — the parallel guilt structure. The man's guilt is defined as violation of the neighbor's wife (covenant violation of another man's exclusive marital right). The law's logic is culturally embedded in the honor-community framework, but its concern for protecting the betrothed woman is its ethical core.
25: "But if in the open country a man meets a young woman who is betrothed, and the man seizes her and lies with her, then only the man who lay with her shall die" — the rural rape scenario: only the man is guilty. The absence of potential witnesses and the inability to call for help means the woman is presumed innocent. The law's context-sensitivity creates the non-consensual/consensual distinction through geographic inference — a primitive but genuine attempt to adjudicate rape versus consensual sex in a legal system without forensic evidence.
26: "But you shall do nothing to the young woman; she has committed no offense punishable by death" — the explicit innocence declaration: ein lana'ar chet mavet (there is no sin of death in the young woman). The legal protection of the rape victim from punishment is explicit and important. The law analogizes to murder: a man who attacks another in a field has no witnesses; the attacked person is the victim. The analogy asserts the rural rape victim is the equivalent of a murder victim — the party against whom the offense is committed, not a participant in the offense.
27: "For he found her in the open country, and though the betrothed young woman cried for help there was no one to rescue her" — the assumed-cry inference: in the rural case, the law assumes the cry for help was made even though no one heard it. The legal system extends benefit of the doubt to the woman in the rural scenario: absence of witnesses to a cry does not prove the absence of a cry. The asymmetry between the city and country rulings shows the law making probabilistic rather than moralistic inferences.
28: "If a man meets a virgin who is not betrothed, and seizes her and lies with her, and they are found" — the non-betrothed virgin rape: a different legal scenario with a different outcome. This case involves a woman with no existing marital claim — the legal consequence differs from the adultery-adjacent betrothal cases above.
29: Then the man shall give fifty shekels of silver to the father and she shall be his wife, and he may not divorce her all his days, because he has violated her — the non-betrothed rape resolution: marriage + fine, no right to divorce. The law is criticized as punishing the victim through mandatory marriage to her rapist. Its ancient context: the law protected the woman from social destitution (an unmarried non-virgin had few options in the ancient world) by requiring the man to provide permanent marital support. The no-divorce provision protects her from a second abandonment after violation. The law is not an ideal but a protection within a specific cultural-economic context.
30: "A man shall not take his father's wife, so that he does not uncover his father's nakedness" — the incest prohibition: the father's wife (stepmother or secondary wife) is covered by the same uncovering-prohibition as Leviticus 18:8. Gilah kenaf aviv — uncovering the corner/wing of his father. 1 Corinthians 5:1-5: Paul addresses the Corinthian case of a man living with his father's wife as requiring excommunication — "a kind of sexual immorality that is not tolerated even among pagans." Paul's application of Deuteronomy's standard to the Gentile church shows the torah-principle operating as moral baseline even in the new covenant context.
